title: Backend Profiling in Node.js with DevTools and Autocannon
summary: A practical Node.js backend profiling workflow using inspect mode, Chrome DevTools, Autocannon, flame graphs, and a CPU-heavy endpoint.
body:
# Backend Profiling in Node.js with DevTools and Autocannon

Backend profiling is often more predictable than frontend profiling. The server environment is controlled, hardware is known, and the same endpoint can be tested repeatedly under load.

That makes backend profiling a good place to build the habit of measuring before optimizing.

## Backend Profiling Goals

For an API, profiling usually tries to answer a few questions:

- Which endpoint is slow?
- Is the request waiting on IO or using CPU?
- Which function consumes the most time?
- Does performance improve after the fix?

The goal is not just to find slow code. The goal is to connect a user-visible problem to a measurable cause.

## Useful Tools

For Node.js, a simple profiling setup can include:

- Chrome DevTools for CPU profiles and flame graphs
- Node.js inspect mode for connecting DevTools to the process
- Autocannon for repeatable HTTP load testing
- Application logs for request duration

## Start Node.js With Inspect Mode

Run the server with the inspector enabled:

```bash
node --inspect server.js
```

Then open Chrome and go to `chrome://inspect`. Your Node.js process should appear as a remote target. From there, open DevTools and use the Performance tab to record CPU activity while the endpoint is under load.

## Generate Load With Autocannon

A single browser request is rarely enough to show meaningful backend behavior. Use a load generator so the endpoint does enough work to profile.

```bash
autocannon -d 10 -c 10 http://localhost:3000/cpu
```

This runs for 10 seconds with 10 concurrent connections.

## Example CPU-Heavy Endpoint

A deliberately slow recursive Fibonacci function is a useful demo because it creates obvious CPU pressure.

```js
function fibonacci(n) {
  if (n <= 1) return n;

  return fibonacci(n - 1) + fibonacci(n - 2);
}
```

If an endpoint calls this with a high enough number, Node.js spends most of its time computing instead of handling other requests.

## Reading the Flame Graph

In a CPU profile, wide frames usually matter more than deep frames. A wide function means the process spent a lot of sampled time there.

If `fibonacci` dominates the flame graph, the bottleneck is not routing, JSON response creation, or the HTTP framework. The bottleneck is CPU computation.

## Fixing CPU Pressure

The fix depends on the real workload. Options include:

- Use a better algorithm
- Cache repeated results
- Move expensive work to a background job
- Move CPU-heavy work to worker threads

Worker threads can help when the work must happen in Node.js but should not block the event loop.

## Measure Before and After

Run the same Autocannon test before and after the change. Compare throughput, latency percentiles, and error rate.

A performance fix is only complete when the measurement improves under the same conditions.
